title: Trial Lawyers Lobby Against Autonomous Vehicles - Marginal REVOLUTION
gist: Large-scale Waymo/Swiss Re data shows driverless vehicles are dramatically safer than human drivers, yet trial lawyers and some Democrats have lobbied against autonomous-vehicle legislation for nearly a decade; Alex Tabarrok argues mandatory no-fault insurance, not product-liability litigation, is the better safety and compensation mechanism.

insights.claim: Over 220 million miles driven across Los Angeles, San Francisco, Phoenix, Austin, and Atlanta, Waymo's driverless vehicles have shown 94% fewer serious injuries, 82% fewer air bag deployments, and 93% fewer pedestrian injuries than matched human driving, per a joint analysis with Swiss Re.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Large-scale, transparent real-world data supports a bullish safety case for AV deployment at scale, relevant to AV insurers, regulators, and robotaxi operators.
insights.locator: The latest data show that over 220 million miles driven

insights.claim: Roughly 37,000-40,000 Americans die in auto accidents every year, the baseline risk pool that autonomous vehicle safety improvements would address.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Sizes the potential public-health and liability-cost upside from AV adoption.

themes.theme: Trial lawyers as the chief opponent of AVs

insights.claim: Despite thousands of lives annually at stake, the most prominent opponent of autonomous vehicle legislation is the trial lawyers' lobby, the American Association for Justice, which has fought AV legislation for nearly a decade.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Identifies a concentrated, well-funded political blocker of AV rollout that investors in AV/robotaxi companies should track in state and federal legislative fights.
insights.locator: Remarkably the trial lawyers saw the writing on the wall

insights.claim: Some Democrats have joined trial lawyers in opposing AV legislation out of concern for labor displacement, specifically demanding that heavy trucks be excluded from AV rules.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Trucking-specific carve-outs are a likely near-term legislative outcome, a headwind for autonomous trucking companies specifically even as passenger AVs advance.
insights.locator: They have been joined by Democrats worried about labor

insights.claim: US auto insurance liability and PIP/MedPay payouts run on the order of $180-220 billion annually, the revenue pool trial lawyers are motivated to defend by retaining the right to sue car manufacturers under product liability.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Quantifies the financial incentive behind litigation-lobby opposition to AVs -- a durable interest group that won't disappear even as safety data improves.
insights.locator: The trial lawyers earn a huge amount litigating

themes.theme: Insurance, not tort, as the right mechanism

insights.claim: Tabarrok argues product liability litigation is not a useful safety device for autonomous vehicles, and that the better solution is mandatory insurance on every car regardless of who or what is driving.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: A clear policy thesis investors in AV/insurtech can use to model likely regulatory outcomes and which business models (insurance-led vs. litigation-exposed) benefit.
insights.locator: In my view, product liability isn't useful

insights.claim: Waymo vehicles are already insured at $5 million in liability coverage per vehicle, far higher than typical human-driver coverage levels.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Shows AV operators are already voluntarily internalizing risk at levels well above the human baseline, undercutting the case that AVs are under-insured relative to their risk.
insights.locator: Waymo vehicles are already insured at $5 million

insights.claim: The UK's Automated and Electric Vehicles Act 2018 offers a working model: a single insurer covers the vehicle whether human or automated system is driving, the victim is compensated directly without proving product defect, and the insurer then subrogates against the manufacturer if the software was at fault.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
entities: UK Automated and Electric Vehicles Act 2018
insights.so_what: A concrete legislative template that US policymakers and AV insurers could adopt; investors should watch for similar US state/federal proposals.
insights.locator: The UK's Automated and Electric Vehicles Act 2018

insights.claim: Under the UK-style insurance model, victims get paid faster and manufacturers still face the cost of their defects through insurer recoveries and premiums, while high-transaction-cost litigation between manufacturers and victims is replaced by efficient insurer-versus-manufacturer bargaining between repeat players.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Argues for a structurally cheaper, faster claims-resolution system that would compress legal costs baked into current auto-liability pricing.
insights.locator: Victims get paid fast, manufacturers face the cost

insights.claim: Insurance deters accidents better than tort litigation because fleet data lets insurers set precise experience-based rates, turning them into continuous safety regulators, whereas litigation delivers a noisy, lagged, lottery-like signal dependent on jury and locale rather than actual safety.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Makes the case that insurance markets, not courts, are the efficient long-run regulator of AV safety -- a bullish signal for insurtech/telematics business models tied to AV fleets.
insights.locator: The great thing about this system is that insurance

insights.claim: Tabarrok argues insurers, not tort lawyers, should be left to price the risk of Tesla robotaxis and FSD (Full Self-Driving) drivers, since Tesla's safety data is murkier than Waymo's but insurers will still internalize the externality more efficiently than litigation would.
insights.speaker: Alex Tabarrok
insights.so_what: Flags Tesla's less transparent safety data as a specific pricing/regulatory risk relative to Waymo, and points to insurance markets as the mechanism that will eventually discipline Tesla's AV risk pricing.
insights.quote: Let the insurers decide how much to charge Tesla robotaxis and FSD drivers--they will internalize the externality far better than tort lawyers.
insights.locator: We have the best data on Waymo, Tesla data is murkier

top_entities: UK Automated and Electric Vehicles Act 2018
